title: Apple GPU Architecture & MPS Basics
description: Master GPU acceleration on Apple Silicon with Metal Performance Shaders. Learn unified memory, device-agnostic patterns, the fp64 trap, and why MPS differs from CUDA at the hardware level.

sections.title: Why GPU Acceleration
sections.content:
### Why GPU Acceleration Matters Modern machine learning relies on **massive matrix multiplication operations**. A single transformer attention layer performs operations like `(batch, seq_len, hidden_dim) @ (hidden_dim, hidden_dim)` — potentially billions of floating-point operations per forward pass. CPUs are fantastic at sequential logic and branching. GPUs excel at the same operation applied to thousands of elements in parallel. GPU hardware dedicates transistors to arithmetic units rather than instruction caches, making them dramatically faster for bulk linear algebra. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU (sequential logic)"] CPU_C["A few cores
~10s of threads
Wide ALUs, big caches"] end subgraph CUDA["NVIDIA GPU (CUDA)"] VRAM["Discrete VRAM (~80 GB)"] SM["1000s of cores"] VRAM -- "PCIe transfer
(explicit .cuda())" --> SM end subgraph MPS["Apple Silicon (MPS)"] UM["Unified memory
(CPU + GPU share RAM)"] GPUC["GPU cores +
Neural Engine"] UM -- "implicit, zero-copy" --> GPUC end Code["torch.tensor(...)"] --> CPU_C Code --> VRAM Code --> UM ``` For matmul-heavy workloads like training a transformer, well-utilized GPUs are typically **50–100x faster than a CPU**, and high-end datacenter GPUs can push that gap further. The exact speedup depends heavily on memory layout, batch size, and how saturated the arithmetic units actually are — never trust a single peak-FLOPS number on a spec sheet. ### What is MPS? **Metal Performance Shaders (MPS)** is Apple's GPU compute framework, analogous to NVIDIA's CUDA on their GPUs. PyTorch's MPS backend dispatches tensor ops to the Apple Silicon GPU cores via Metal. It leverages the unified-memory architecture and the GPU's wide SIMD units, typically achieving a meaningful fraction of high-end NVIDIA GPU performance for typical workloads while consuming far less power. ```python import torch # Check if MPS is available on this Apple Silicon Mac print(torch.backends.mps.is_available()) # True on M1/M2/M3+, False on Intel/older Macs # Move a tensor to MPS device device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu") x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device) print(x.device) # device(type='mps') ``` Note: PyTorch's MPS backend runs on the **GPU cores**, not on the Apple Neural Engine (ANE). The ANE is a separate accelerator only reachable through Core ML — covered in module 5 of this course. If you want maximum native Apple-Silicon performance from Python directly, see Apple's **MLX** framework (module 4).

sections.title: MPS vs CUDA
sections.content:
### Key Differences from CUDA

MPS is powerful but has gotchas compared to CUDA:

| Feature | CUDA | MPS |
|---------|------|-----|
| **Float64 (fp64)** | Fully supported | Not supported; will error |
| **Device transfer syntax** | Both should use `.to(device)`. `.cuda()` exists historically but is no longer recommended. | Same — use `.to(device)`. There is no `.mps()` shorthand by design. |
| **Unified memory** | Optional, explicit | Default; no explicit sync needed |
| **Debugging** | cuda-gdb, Nsight, profilers | Limited tooling |
| **Vendor** | NVIDIA only | Apple Silicon only |
| **Power efficiency** | ~300W for A100 | ~10–30W typical for M-series |
| **Memory bandwidth** | High-end datacenter GPUs: 2–3+ TB/s (A100, H100) | Apple Silicon: ~100 GB/s base · ~400 GB/s Max · ~800 GB/s Ultra |

### Why These Differences Exist

CUDA assumes discrete GPUs with separate memory (VRAM), requiring explicit CPU↔GPU transfers. Apple Silicon has **unified memory**: the same physical RAM serves CPU and GPU, so transfers are implicit and zero-copy. This design choice trades explicit control for developer convenience, and has very different performance characteristics: discrete GPUs win on raw bandwidth, unified memory wins on transfer-free interop.

### The fp64 Gotcha (The #1 Source of Runtime Errors)

Creating an fp64 (double-precision float) tensor on MPS will crash with a cryptic RuntimeError:

```python
# Incorrect: this raises RuntimeError on MPS
x = torch.randn(100, dtype=torch.float64, device=torch.device("mps"))
# RuntimeError: float64 is not supported on MPS

# Correct: always use float32 (default) on MPS
x = torch.randn(100, dtype=torch.float32, device=torch.device("mps"))
# or simply (float32 is default):
x = torch.randn(100, device=torch.device("mps"))
```

**Why does this happen?** The Apple Silicon GPU lacks 64-bit floating-point hardware units; only the CPU has hardware fp64. The MPS backend therefore refuses fp64 rather than silently downcasting and corrupting your numerics.

This often happens accidentally when:
- A function defaults to float64 (some scientific libs do this)
- You load a model trained on CUDA (which might have parameters stored as float64)
- A library internally promotes to float64 for numerical stability
- You call `.double()` on a model without thinking

**Solutions:**

```python
# Solution 1: explicit float32 at model creation
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float()
model = model.to("mps")

# Solution 2: load checkpoint to CPU, downcast, then move
checkpoint = torch.load("model.pt", map_location="cpu")
model.load_state_dict(checkpoint)
model = model.float().to("mps")

# Solution 3: cast stray fp64 parameters in-place before moving
with torch.no_grad():
    for param in model.parameters():
        if param.dtype == torch.float64:
            param.copy_(param.float())
model = model.to("mps")
```

A related dtype trap: `numpy` defaults to `float64` for many constructors. `torch.from_numpy(np.ones(3))` gives you a float64 tensor — fine on CPU, broken on MPS. Always `.float()` before moving.

sections.title: Device-Agnostic Patterns
sections.content:
### Device-Agnostic Code Pattern

**Best practice**: write code that works on any device without hardcoding `"mps"` or `"cuda"`.

```python
# Pattern 1: Try MPS, fall back to CPU
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device)
model = MyModel().to(device)  # .to() works on any device

# Pattern 2: Full hierarchy (CUDA > MPS > CPU)
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = "cuda"
elif torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = "mps"
else:
    device = "cpu"
device = torch.device(device)
```

### MPS Fallback: When Operations Aren't Supported

Some operations don't yet have an MPS implementation. PyTorch can fall back to CPU automatically:

```bash
PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 python train.py
```

This environment variable tells PyTorch: "if an op fails on MPS, silently run it on CPU instead." Useful during development; avoid in production because it can introduce silent CPU↔GPU round-trips that wreck performance. Better is to identify the offending op explicitly:

```python
if device.type == "mps":
    result = some_unsupported_op(x.cpu()).to(device)
else:
    result = some_unsupported_op(x)
```

### `torch.compile` on MPS

`torch.compile` (the TorchInductor JIT) is supported on MPS but coverage is partial. Many ops will compile and run faster; others will fall back to eager mode silently. Treat compile speedups on MPS as a bonus, not a guarantee — benchmark the compiled vs. uncompiled version of your specific model before relying on it.

### Reproducibility and Seeding

For deterministic experiments, seed every RNG you use, including the MPS device and any non-PyTorch libraries:

```python
import random, numpy as np, torch

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)        # seeds CPU and (where applicable) all CUDA devices
torch.mps.manual_seed(SEED)    # MPS-specific RNG
# torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)  # optional, may slow training
```

Bit-exact reproducibility on GPU is **not free**: many fast kernels use non-deterministic reductions. Setting `use_deterministic_algorithms(True)` forces deterministic implementations where available and raises an error elsewhere — useful for debugging, costly for throughput.

### Unified Memory: Why It Matters

Apple Silicon has **unified memory**: CPU and GPU access the same RAM without explicit copying. CUDA requires you to manage PCIe transfers (`.to("cuda")`, `.to("cpu")`); MPS handles this transparently, reducing boilerplate and a whole class of "tensor on wrong device" bugs.

The tradeoff is bandwidth: high-end NVIDIA datacenter GPUs deliver 2–3+ TB/s of memory bandwidth; Apple Silicon ranges from roughly 100 GB/s on base M-series chips to ~800 GB/s on Ultra-tier parts. That is why memory-bound operations on MPS can be slower than on top-end NVIDIA hardware even when peak FLOPS look comparable.

### Memory Introspection on MPS

When training crashes with OOM, you need to know what your process is actually using. The `torch.mps` namespace exposes a small but essential API:

```python
import torch

# Bytes currently allocated to live tensors
print(torch.mps.current_allocated_memory())
# Bytes the driver has actually reserved (>= current_allocated, includes cached blocks)
print(torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory())

# Free the cached (unused) blocks back to the system; does NOT free live tensors
torch.mps.empty_cache()
```

`current_allocated_memory()` is your live working set; `driver_allocated_memory()` includes the allocator's free list. The gap between them is fragmentation. `empty_cache()` is useful when interleaving heavy training with other GPU users on the same machine — but it cannot rescue a process that genuinely needs more memory than it has.

sections.title: MPS Pitfalls & Synchronization
sections.content:
### Pitfall 1: Mixing dtypes Between Layers
```python
# Incorrect: creates dtype mismatch
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float().to("mps")
model.weight = nn.Parameter(model.weight.double())  # weight now float64
x = torch.randn(1, 10, dtype=torch.float32, device="mps")
y = model(x)  # Will fail: can't multiply float32 input with float64 weight

# Correct: keep everything float32
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).float().to("mps")
x = torch.randn(1, 10, dtype=torch.float32, device="mps")
y = model(x)  # Works; both are float32
```

### Pitfall 2: Forgetting to Move Data to Device
```python
# Incorrect: model on MPS, data on CPU
device = torch.device("mps")
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device)
x = torch.randn(1, 10)  # On CPU by default
y = model(x)  # RuntimeError: expected all tensors on the same device

# Correct: move both model and data
device = torch.device("mps")
model = nn.Linear(10, 5).to(device)
x = torch.randn(1, 10).to(device)  # Move data too
y = model(x)  # Works
```

### Pitfall 3: Not Synchronizing Before Timing
```python
# Incorrect: GPU operations are async; the timer fires before the GPU finishes
import time
start = time.perf_counter()
x = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
y = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
z = x @ y  # GPU operation queued, not finished yet
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Time: {end - start}")  # Reports launch time, not actual compute time

# Correct: synchronize before stopping the clock
import time
start = time.perf_counter()
x = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
y = torch.randn(10000, 10000, device="mps")
z = x @ y
torch.mps.synchronize()  # Wait for GPU to finish
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Time: {end - start}")
```

GPU launches are queued asynchronously: the Python-side call returns as soon as the kernel is enqueued, not when it finishes. Without `torch.mps.synchronize()` (or `torch.cuda.synchronize()` on CUDA) your timer measures launch latency, not compute time, and can be off by orders of magnitude.
sections.code:
import torch
import time

# 1. Check MPS availability
print(f"MPS available: {torch.backends.mps.is_available()}")

# 2. Create device-agnostic setup
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

# 3. Create and move tensors
x = torch.randn(1000, 1000, device=device)
print(f"Tensor device: {x.device}, dtype: {x.dtype}")

# 4. Device transfer with .to()
model = torch.nn.Linear(100, 10)
model = model.to(device)
print(f"Model moved to: {next(model.parameters()).device}")

# 5. Avoid fp64 on MPS (demonstrate error gracefully)
try:
    bad_tensor = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float64, device=device)
except (RuntimeError, TypeError) as e:
    print(f"Error (expected on MPS): {str(e)[:80]}...")

# 6. Always use float32 on MPS
good_tensor = torch.randn(10, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
print(f"Created fp32 tensor: {good_tensor.shape}, dtype: {good_tensor.dtype}")

# 7. Memory introspection (MPS-only)
if device.type == "mps":
    print(f"Allocated: {torch.mps.current_allocated_memory() / 1e6:.2f} MB")
    print(f"Driver-reserved: {torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory() / 1e6:.2f} MB")

# 8. Benchmark: simple matmul
x = torch.randn(2000, 2000, device=device)
y = torch.randn(2000, 2000, device=device)
if device.type == "mps":
    torch.mps.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
z = x @ y
if device.type == "mps":
    torch.mps.synchronize()
end = time.perf_counter()
print(f"Matmul (2000x2000) took {(end-start)*1000:.2f}ms")

quiz.question: How do you safely check if MPS is available on the current machine?
quiz.explanation: torch.backends.mps.is_available() is the official PyTorch API. The first option is incorrect (no .mps module at top level). The third doesn't exist. The fourth checks the OS but not MPS specifically (Intel Macs run darwin too).

quiz.question: What happens if you try to create a float64 tensor on MPS?
options: It creates the tensor but operations are slow
quiz.explanation: MPS hardware does not have float64 support. PyTorch will raise a RuntimeError. It does not auto-convert or silently fall back without PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 set.

quiz.question: What is a key advantage of Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture?
options: Data is faster to access than on NVIDIA GPUs
You don't need to explicitly manage data transfers between CPU and GPU
It automatically converts fp64 to fp32
It provides better debugging tools than CUDA
quiz.explanation: Unified memory means CPU and GPU share the same address space, so you don't need explicit .to("cuda") transfers like on NVIDIA. The OS handles copying transparently. (Speed can be slower than NVIDIA on memory-bound ops, and debugging tooling is limited.)

quiz.question: What environment variable enables CPU fallback for unsupported MPS operations?
quiz.explanation: PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 tells PyTorch to fall back to CPU for unsupported operations. Useful for development but should be avoided in production due to silent performance degradation.

quiz.question: Why do you need torch.mps.synchronize() before stopping a benchmark timer?
options: To free GPU memory before timing
GPU operations are queued asynchronously; without sync, you measure launch time, not compute time
To force the model into evaluation mode
It's only required on CUDA, not on MPS
quiz.explanation: GPU launches return as soon as the kernel is enqueued, not when it finishes. Without torch.mps.synchronize() your timer measures launch latency rather than actual compute time, often off by orders of magnitude.

exercise.title: MPS Device Benchmark
exercise.description:
Create tensors on CPU and MPS, benchmark matrix multiplication at increasing sizes, and report the speedup factor.

**Requirements:**
- Create square matrices of sizes: 256, 512, 1024, 2048
- For each size, benchmark matmul on both CPU and MPS
- Use `torch.mps.synchronize()` before measuring MPS time (ensures all GPU ops complete)
- Report speedup as CPU_time / MPS_time for each size
- Handle the case where MPS is unavailable gracefully
- Use consistent number of iterations (5) for all sizes

**Hints:**
- Use `time.perf_counter()` for timing (more precise than `time.time()`)
- Warm up the GPU with one iteration before timing
- Run at least 5 iterations per size to reduce noise
- MPS synchronization is critical; without it, timers fire before GPU finishes
- Print results in a clear table format for easy comparison
exercise.starterCode:
import torch
import time

# Note: torch.device(None) raises TypeError, so build the device only when MPS is
# actually available and fall back to plain CPU otherwise.
if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device_mps = torch.device("mps")
else:
    device_mps = torch.device("cpu")
device_cpu = torch.device("cpu")

def benchmark_matmul(size, device, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark matrix multiplication on a given device."""
    # Create random matrices
    a = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)
    b = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)

    # Warm up (1 iteration)
    _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()

    # Timed iterations
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()
    end = time.perf_counter()

    return (end - start) / num_iters

# TODO: Test matmul at sizes [256, 512, 1024, 2048]
# TODO: Compute speedup (CPU time / MPS time) for each size
# TODO: Print results in a table with columns: Size, CPU (ms), MPS (ms), Speedup (x)
# TODO: Handle case where MPS is unavailable
exercise.solution:
import torch
import time

mps_available = torch.backends.mps.is_available()
device_mps = torch.device("mps") if mps_available else torch.device("cpu")
device_cpu = torch.device("cpu")

def benchmark_matmul(size, device, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark matrix multiplication on a given device."""
    a = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)
    b = torch.randn(size, size, device=device)

    # Warm up
    _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()

    # Timed iterations
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        _ = torch.matmul(a, b)
    if device.type == "mps":
        torch.mps.synchronize()
    end = time.perf_counter()

    return (end - start) / num_iters

# Benchmark at different sizes
sizes = [256, 512, 1024, 2048]
print("\nMatrix Multiplication Benchmark (avg time per iter)\n")
print(f"{'Size':<8} {'CPU (ms)':<12} {'MPS (ms)':<12} {'Speedup':<10}")
print("-" * 42)

if not mps_available:
    print("MPS not available on this device; running CPU baseline only.")
    for size in sizes:
        cpu_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_cpu, num_iters=5) * 1000
        print(f"{size:<8} {cpu_time:<12.3f} {'n/a':<12} {'n/a':<10}")
else:
    for size in sizes:
        cpu_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_cpu, num_iters=5) * 1000
        mps_time = benchmark_matmul(size, device_mps, num_iters=5) * 1000
        speedup = cpu_time / mps_time
        print(f"{size:<8} {cpu_time:<12.3f} {mps_time:<12.3f} {speedup:<10.2f}x")

print("\nSpeedup > 1.0 means MPS is faster. Speedup may vary based on hardware.")
